Understanding MLB Division Winner Odds

MLB division odds represent the betting market where fans can wager on which Major League Baseball teams they predict will win each of the six divisions: the American League East, Central, and West, and the National League East, Central, and West.

Here's a step-by-step guide on understanding MLB division odds:

  • Understanding the odds: The odds for each team to win their division are presented with either a plus (+) or minus (-) sign. The plus sign indicates an underdog and the minus sign indicates a favorite. For a favorite (e.g., -150), the number represents how much you need to bet to win $100, while for an underdog (e.g., +200), the number shows how much you'd win if you bet $100. For example, if a team's odds to win the division are +300, a $100 bet would yield a $300 profit if that team wins the division. If another team's odds are -200, you'd need to wager $200 to win $100. The lower the number (or higher if it's a negative), the more likely the team is expected to win.

  • Choosing your team: Pick the team you believe will win their division. Factors to consider include team performance, player health, head-to-head records against division rivals, and strength of the remaining schedule.

  • Placing your bet: Once you've chosen the team you think will win, you can place your wager amount in the betslip. The potential payout is calculated by multiplying your stake by the odds.

  • Waiting for the result: Division winners bets are paid out at the end of the season when all the winners are official, so this type of bet requires patience. If your chosen team finishes atop their division, your bet is a winner.

MLB division odds can fluctuate throughout the season based on factors such as team performance, player injuries, trades, and other relevant news. As such, it's essential to stay updated with the MLB season and adjust your betting strategy accordingly.

Popular Types of MLB Betting Odds

When betting on MLB, you'll find several popular bet types at FanDuel Sportsbook. Here's a quick rundown of the most common ways to bet on Major League Baseball:

  • MLB Moneylines: The most straightforward bet - pick which team wins the game outright. A negative number (e.g., -122) indicates the favorite and how much you need to wager to win $100. A positive number (e.g., +104) denotes the underdog and the profit on a $100 bet.
  • MLB Run Line (Spread): In baseball, the Spread is typically set at -1.5 or +1.5 runs. A favorite with a -1.5 run line must win by 2 or more runs. An underdog with a +1.5 run line can either win outright or lose by just 1 run for your bet to cash.
  • MLB Totals (Over/Under): Bet on the combined number of runs scored by both teams. FanDuel sets a specific number and you decide if the final score will go Over or Under that total.
  • MLB Player Props: These wagers focus on individual player performances; pitcher strikeouts, total bases, hits, RBIs, and more. Props can be found in the "More Wagers" section of our odds tables.
  • MLB Futures: Long-term bets on season-ending outcomes including World Series winner, pennant winners, division winners, and individual awards like MVP and Cy Young.
  • MLB Parlays and Same Game Parlays: Combine multiple selections into a single Parlay for increased odds and potential payouts. A Same Game Parlay allows you to combine multiple bets from a single matchup.

How Do You Read MLB Odds?

Reading baseball betting odds is straightforward once you know the basics. The favorite is always listed with a minus sign (-) and the underdog with a plus sign (+). For example, if the Yankees are -122, you need to bet $122 to win $100. If the Giants are +104, a $100 bet returns $104 in profit. The Run Line works similarly, a -1.5 favorite must win by 2+ runs, while a +1.5 underdog can lose by 1 run and still cover. New to sports betting? MLB Betting FAQs


Does Extra Innings Count Toward My MLB Bets?

Yes. For nearly every MLB market: Moneylines, Run Lines, Totals, all statistics include any extra innings unless the market header clearly states otherwise.

What Happens to Player Prop Bets if a Player Doesn't Play?

If a starting pitcher or position player does not play, all bets on that player will be void. Bets stand once a player takes the field or enters the game.

What Is the Minimum Game Duration for MLB Bets to Stand?

MLB games must reach at least 5 innings (or 4.5 innings if the home team is leading) for bets to stand. If a game is suspended before that threshold and doesn't resume within 24 hours, bets will be void unless the result of a market has been clearly determined.

What Happens if Two Players Tie in an MLB Prop?

When a prop ends in a tie, FanDuel applies dead-heat rules. Your stake is divided by the number of tied winners and settled at the original odds, so you still get paid, just on a proportionally reduced stake.

Which Statistics Does FanDuel Use to Grade MLB Wagers?

All bets are settled using results and box-score data published by Major League Baseball's official governing body and its data supplier. Once FanDuel grades a market, subsequent stat corrections do not alter the outcome of your wager.

  • MLB Totals (Over/Under): Bet on the total number of combined runs in the game. If the Over/Under is set at 8.5: a 5-4 final score (9 runs) hits the Over, while a 4-3 score (7 runs) hits the Under.

MLB Odds and Betting FAQs


  • How many innings must be completed for my moneyline bet to have action?

    A moneyline wager becomes official as soon as five innings are in the books, or 4.5 innings if the home team is already leading when the bottom half of the fifth begins. If the game is called or suspended before that point and never resumes within the 48-hour window, all moneyline bets are void.

  • I bet the Run Line (-1.5/+1.5). What happens if weather stops the game early?

    Run-line (spread) and game-total markets need a minimum of nine innings (8.5 if the home club is up) to grade. If the game is shortened before that threshold and the outcome of the wager hasn’t already been decided, the bet is void and stakes are returned.

  • What’s the difference between “Action” and “Listed Pitchers” on MLB odds?

    Action (FanDuel default) means your moneyline bet stands at the price you locked in, even if either scheduled starter is scratched.

    If there is one listed pitcher, it is required that your chosen starter to pitch; the other side may change. If your pitcher doesn’t start, the wager is void. When both pitchers are listed, both announced starters must throw the first pitch. Any change cancels the bet. All other markets (totals, run lines, props) are always “action.”

  • Do extra innings count toward my game bets?

    Yes. Unless the House Rules or market description says otherwise, extra innings apply to settle moneyline, run-line, total-runs and most prop wagers (e.g., “Team to score last”)

  • What if the game is suspended and finishes the next day?

    If play is halted and resumes within 48 hours of the originally scheduled first pitch, all open bets carry over and are settled when the game ends. If completion is scheduled for more than 48 hours later (regular-season) or beyond 90 days in the postseason, any wager not already decided at suspension is void.

3073.footer:
Sportsbook Odds / MLB Odds / World Baseball Classic Betting Odds

2026 World Baseball Classic Winner and MVP Odds

Track the latest 2026 World Baseball Classic odds and see which teams are favored to win the tournament. The World Baseball Classic brings together top national teams from around the world, creating exciting futures markets for bettors throughout the event. At FanDuel Sportsbook, you can explore WBC winner odds, follow tournament favorites, and place bets on the team you think will take home the title. In addition to championship futures, bettors can also check World Baseball Classic MVP odds, giving you the opportunity to back standout players expected to dominate the tournament. Odds will update throughout the competition as teams advance and matchups develop.

How World Baseball Classic Odds Work

World Baseball Classic betting odds show the potential payout for picking the correct outcome in a futures market, such as the tournament winner or MVP. Futures bets allow you to place a wager before or during the event on outcomes that will be decided later in the tournament. Odds are typically reflect both the likelihood of an outcome and the potential return on a wager. As the tournament unfolds, sportsbooks may adjust WBC odds based on team performance, matchups, and betting activity. Monitoring these updates can help bettors make more informed decisions throughout the competition.
